parentQuestion: The brain stem consists of the ___.

scaffolds.q: The brainstem connects the brain to the spinal cord.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The brainstem is the bridge between your thinking brain and your body. All signals traveling up or down must pass through it.

scaffolds.q: The brainstem controls vital functions like breathing and heart rate.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The brainstem runs your autopilot - breathing, heart rate, blood pressure, sleeping, waking. These happen without conscious thought.

scaffolds.q: The cerebrum is the large thinking brain that sits ON TOP of the brainstem.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The cerebrum (the wrinkled dome) sits above the brainstem. The brainstem is the stalk underneath, not part of the cerebrum.

scaffolds.q: The cerebellum is attached to the BACK of the brainstem.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The cerebellum hangs off the back of the brainstem like a small cauliflower. It's connected to the brainstem but is NOT part of it.

scaffolds.q: The midbrain is the upper part of the brainstem.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The midbrain is the most superior (highest) part of the brainstem. It contains reflex centers for vision and hearing.

scaffolds.q: The pons is the middle part of the brainstem.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The pons sits between the midbrain and medulla. 'Pons' means bridge - it relays signals and helps control breathing.

scaffolds.q: The medulla oblongata is the lowest part of the brainstem, continuous with the spinal cord.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The medulla is where the brainstem becomes the spinal cord. It controls heart rate and breathing - damage it, and you die.

scaffolds.q: The brainstem has three parts: midbrain, pons, and medulla oblongata.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! From top to bottom: midbrain → pons → medulla. Three structures stacked like a neural totem pole. The cerebrum and cerebellum are NOT part of the brainstem.

scaffolds.q: The brainstem consists of the midbrain, pons, and medulla - NOT the cerebrum or cerebellum.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The answer is midbrain + pons + medulla. The cerebrum sits on top, the cerebellum hangs on back - neither is part of the brainstem itself.
